writer_persona: a showrunner for character-driven, relationship-first serial drama in the vein of prestige ensemble TV, specializing in slow-burn family sagas where old money hides older debts
style_guidelines: Cut between threads every scene or two — never let one storyline dominate more than two consecutive sections.
Every scene must be legible from dialogue and narration alone — no visual descriptions the audience can't hear.
Let characters lie fluently and specifically; a good lie contains just enough truth to be survivable.
Withhold, don't explain: never let a character's dialogue or the narration reveal more than that character's own knowledge slice allows.
Escalate stakes through compounding secrets, not revelations from nowhere — every twist should trace back to something already planted.
Keep confrontations clipped and interruption-heavy; let silences and unfinished sentences carry as much weight as what's said aloud.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

MARGUERITE_VANTRY.default_style: poised and controlled, warmth stretched thin over steel; speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English
MARGUERITE_VANTRY.description: chair of the Vantry Auction House; came to Esmond Harbor from Hong Kong at nineteen and married into the family, spending fifty years becoming more Vantry than the Vantrys — the fiercest guardian of a family myth she only half knows is false

JULIAN_VANTRY.default_style: outwardly composed golden-boy charm fraying at the edges into clipped, evasive answers
JULIAN_VANTRY.description: Marguerite's son, CEO and lead auctioneer; the first to notice the ledgers don't add up, and the one whose secrecy in chasing that discovery is quietly wrecking his marriage

NORA_VANTRY.default_style: precise, professional appraiser's diction thinning into hurt; speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English
NORA_VANTRY.description: Julian's wife and the house's chief appraiser; Korean-American, raised between Seoul and the coast, the first to spot the ledger inconsistencies during a routine insurance re-appraisal — and the last to learn why her husband has been lying to her

SIMON_LIN.default_style: quiet, unhurried, decades of practiced discretion in every pause
SIMON_LIN.description: the auction house's elderly framer and restorer for over fifty years, son of the founding collection's true co-builder; has kept the family's oldest secret out of fear and pragmatism, and is the only person alive who can prove it

ANNA_LIN.default_style: sharp and curious, professionally guarded but privately unmoored; speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English
ANNA_LIN.description: an independent fine-art insurance appraiser hired to reassess the Founding Collection, chasing her own family's fragmentary history on the side without yet knowing the old restorer at the house is her own grandfather

MARCUS_TANG.default_style: smooth, charming, a patient predator's calm; speaks slow, deliberate, very clear English
MARCUS_TANG.description: Hong Kong-born owner of the rival Meridian Auctions; quietly buying up the Vantry family's debt and leverage, driven as much by an inherited grudge from his own grandfather's era as by present-day ambition

VANTRY_AUCTION_HOUSE.name: The Vantry Salesroom
VANTRY_AUCTION_HOUSE.description: A soaring beaux-arts hall of gilt-framed lots, velvet ropes, and chandelier light that makes even scandal look expensive.

ARCHIVE_ROOM.name: The Ledger Archive
ARCHIVE_ROOM.description: A cramped, windowless basement room of locked cabinets and a single desk lamp, where eighty years of paperwork gather dust nobody was supposed to disturb.

RESTORATION_WORKSHOP.name: Simon's Workshop
RESTORATION_WORKSHOP.description: A warm, amber-lit workshop thick with the smell of varnish and glue, half-finished frames stacked against the walls like patient witnesses.

VANTRY_HOUSE.name: The Vantry House
VANTRY_HOUSE.description: A grand seafront estate of family portraits and hushed formal dinners, every room arranged to perform a certainty the family no longer feels.

THE_WATERFRONT.name: Esmond Harbor Waterfront
THE_WATERFRONT.description: A foggy working pier of gulls, old fishing boats, and converted warehouse galleries — the one place in town conversations can happen unheard.

MERIDIAN_AUCTIONS.name: Meridian Auctions
MERIDIAN_AUCTIONS.description: A sleek glass-and-steel rival gallery of clean lines and cold light, built to make the Vantry Salesroom look like a museum of its own decline.

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight

series_intro: Esmond Harbor's Vantry Auction House has spent eighty-seven years selling other people's histories under gleaming chandeliers. Its own history, the family always assumed, was settled — signed, sealed, and appraised long ago. Then a routine insurance re-appraisal turns up a ledger that doesn't add up, a stranger arrives asking careful questions she has her own reasons for asking, and a rival across town starts calling in debts nobody knew the family owed. Nobody in this house — or in the family that was quietly wronged to build it — knows the whole truth yet. That gap is where this story lives.
series_outro: By season's end, the truth of the 1951 forgery is public record, and the Vantry family can no longer pretend the Founding Collection was built on anything but a stolen stake. Julian and Nora's marriage survives, rebuilt on the truth finally being spoken instead of hidden. Simon and Anna Lin choose, at last, to be family in the open rather than in silence. But Marcus Tang's bid for the house is only beaten back for now, not settled for good — and what the Vantry name is worth, once its founding lie is common knowledge, is a question the finale leaves deliberately open.

episodes.theme: A routine insurance re-appraisal of the Founding Collection turns up a signature that doesn't sit right for Nora (The Forged Ledgers), outside appraiser Anna Lin arrives without yet knowing her own family's buried connection to the house (The Hidden Heir), Marcus Tang makes his first courteous approach to Marguerite about a possible sale (The Hostile Buyout), and Julian's newly late nights begin quietly straining his marriage (The Affair That Wasn't).
episodes.outro_note: Nora resolves to bring what she's found straight to Julian in the morning — unaware that the moment she does, the secrets in this family will multiply instead of end.

case.label: The Forged Ledgers

knows: The family history of the Founding Collection as she has always understood it: Augustin Vantry built it alone through shrewd early acquisitions.
believes_falsely: That the founding story of the house is completely true, with nothing in it to guard against — only to celebrate.

knows: Nothing yet about the specific ledger discrepancy — the re-appraisal has only just begun.
believes_falsely: That whatever the re-appraisal turns up will be routine, since it always has been before.

knows: A signature on a 1951 appraisal entry doesn't match Augustin Vantry's handwriting elsewhere in the archive, and the recorded sum looks inflated against comparable lots.
hides: Her full unease about what she's found — she hasn't told anyone yet, wanting to be sure before she raises it.
believes_falsely: That this is most likely a bookkeeping anomaly or a later re-transcription, not evidence of anything deliberate.

knows: The complete truth of the 1938 partnership, the 1951 forgery, and his father Lin Wei-Chen's cheated buyout — and holds the original correspondence and pre-1951 ledger pages that prove it.
hides: All of it, from everyone in the house, out of decades of practiced fear and pragmatism.
believes_falsely: That the discrepancy Nora has only just started noticing won't be traced back this soon — he has underestimated how quickly a professional re-appraisal moves.

knows: She has been assigned by the family's insurer to independently reassess the Founding Collection ahead of a planned museum loan; she has not yet examined the archive herself.
believes_falsely: Nothing yet on this thread — she hasn't touched the ledgers.

case.solution: In 1938, Augustin Vantry partnered with Guangdong-born dealer Lin Wei-Chen to acquire and authenticate the forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art that became the Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection, under a signed agreement giving Lin a 40% stake and his name on every appraisal ledger. In 1951, facing a bank's due-diligence review for an expansion loan, Augustin forged thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal entries — stripping Lin's signature and replacing it with his own, and inflating the recorded appraisal sums by roughly sixty percent above their true 1938 figures. He used the inflated ledger to secure a larger loan than the collection actually justified, then used that loan to buy out Lin's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated valuation, effectively cheating Lin out of the true worth of his share while implicitly threatening to report him — an immigrant with little legal protection at the time — on a fabricated smuggling allegation if he resisted. Lin never publicly contested the buyout, but was quietly kept on staff in a demoted, uncredited restorer's role for the rest of his career and told his son Simon the full truth before he died in 1974. Simon Lin has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968, saying nothing, but has privately kept the original 1938 correspondence and several authentic pre-1951 ledger pages proving exactly what was altered.
objective_facts: The Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection — forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art — was jointly acquired in 1938 by Augustin Vantry and his business partner Lin Wei-Chen under a signed partnership agreement giving Lin a 40% stake.

In 1951, thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal ledger entries were altered: Lin Wei-Chen's signature was removed and replaced with Augustin Vantry's, and the recorded appraisal sums were inflated by approximately sixty percent above the entries' original 1938 figures.
The inflated 1951 ledger was used to secure a bank loan that Augustin Vantry then used to buy out Lin Wei-Chen's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated 1938 valuation.
Lin Wei-Chen remained employed at the auction house afterward in an uncredited restorer role until his death in 1974, and never publicly disputed the buyout.

Lin Wei-Chen's son, Simon Lin, has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968 and privately holds original pre-1951 correspondence and several authentic ledger pages proving the forgery.

case.label: The Hidden Heir

knows: The new outside appraiser's name is Anna Lin — a name and a face that feel unsettlingly familiar the moment she walks into his workshop.
hides: The strange pull he feels toward her, saying nothing about it to anyone.

knows: Fragments of family lore about 'a bad business deal' that cost her great-grandfather Lin Wei-Chen everything, and she carries an old photograph of him beside the Founding Collection in 1938.
hides: This personal research and the photograph from the Vantry family and from her own client — it feels irrelevant to the paid job, and she isn't sure it matters.
believes_falsely: That any family connection to auction houses in general is likely coincidental, generic old-world history, not specifically tied to this house.

case.solution: Anna Lin is Simon Lin's granddaughter and Lin Wei-Chen's great-granddaughter, though she grew up largely unaware of any connection to the Vantry Auction House. Her father, Simon's son, left Esmond Harbor in 1989 after a bitter argument with Simon over his father's lifelong refusal to challenge the Vantrys over the 1951 buyout, and cut off most contact afterward — leaving Anna with only fragments of family lore about 'a bad business deal' that cost her great-grandfather everything. She becomes a fine-art insurance appraiser and is independently assigned by the Vantry family's insurer — not by any design of her own — to reassess the Founding Collection ahead of a planned loan to an overseas museum. Genuinely by coincidence, not conspiracy, she recognizes the name Lin Wei-Chen in the original 1938 partnership paperwork and begins quietly researching her own family history alongside her professional appraisal, not yet realizing that the courteous old restorer at the house, Simon Lin, is her own grandfather. Simon recognizes her almost immediately by name and resemblance, but says nothing.
objective_facts: Anna Lin is the granddaughter of Simon Lin and great-granddaughter of Lin Wei-Chen, though she grew up unaware of any connection to the Vantry Auction House.

Anna Lin's father, Simon Lin's son, left Esmond Harbor in 1989 after a bitter argument with Simon about Simon's decision never to challenge the Vantry family over the 1951 buyout, and cut off most contact afterward.
Anna Lin was hired by the Vantry family's insurer, not by the Vantry family directly, to conduct an independent re-appraisal of the Founding Collection ahead of a planned loan to an overseas museum.
Independent of her professional assignment, Anna Lin has been quietly researching her family's history and possesses an old photograph of Lin Wei-Chen standing beside the Founding Collection in 1938.
Simon Lin recognized Anna Lin's name and resemblance to his own mother within days of her arrival at the auction house, but has not yet told her who he is.

case.label: The Hostile Buyout

knows: He has spent two years covertly acquiring the Vantry family's debt and shareholding leverage, and holds a partial copy of the 1951 ledger's inconsistencies inherited alongside his grandfather Wing-Ho Tang's suspicions.
hides: The full extent of his financial position and his family's grievance from Marguerite, presenting his interest as ordinary business opportunism.
believes_falsely: That he has time to move patiently — he doesn't yet grasp how close Nora's re-appraisal already is to the same discrepancies he's been quietly collecting.

knows: Marcus Tang has made a first, courteous approach about a possible sale or investment in the house.
hides: How much more unsettled the approach left her than she's letting on, even to her own family.
believes_falsely: That Marcus's interest is purely ordinary business ambition, nothing personal or historical.

knows: His mother mentioned, in passing, that Marcus Tang had made some kind of overture.
believes_falsely: That it's a minor, easily ignored courtesy call, not worth his attention.

case.solution: Marcus Tang, owner of the rival Meridian Auctions, has spent two years covertly acquiring the Vantry family's outstanding mortgage debt and buying out three minority-shareholding cousins through shell companies, working toward a controlling position in the house. His motive is only partly business: his grandfather, dealer Wing-Ho Tang, lost a contested 1952 bid against Augustin Vantry that was decided using the fraudulently inflated 1951 ledger valuation, and left behind papers documenting his suspicion of the result — a grievance Marcus inherited along with the family archive. Marcus has since obtained, through a private archive of his own, a partial copy of the 1951 ledger's inconsistencies — enough to suspect fraud, though not the full extent of it that Simon and Anna Lin can actually prove. Rather than exposing the forgery outright, he is using the private threat of exposure to pressure Marguerite into a quiet, deeply discounted sale before the museum-loan re-appraisal forces the truth into the open on its own.
objective_facts: Marcus Tang, owner of the rival Meridian Auctions, has spent the past two years covertly acquiring the Vantry family's outstanding mortgage debt and buying out three minority-shareholding cousins through shell companies.

Marcus Tang's grandfather, dealer Wing-Ho Tang, lost a contested 1952 bid against Augustin Vantry that was decided using the fraudulently inflated 1951 ledger valuation, and left behind papers documenting his suspicion of the result.
Marcus Tang has independently obtained a partial copy of the 1951 ledger's inconsistencies through a private archive, though not the full extent of the forgery that Simon Lin and Anna Lin can prove.
Marcus Tang has made two private, undocumented approaches to Marguerite Vantry proposing an acquisition of the Vantry Auction House substantially below its market value.
Marcus Tang has not yet disclosed his knowledge of the forged ledgers to anyone outside his own staff.

case.label: The Affair That Wasn't

knows: He's been staying late at the house, preoccupied, though nothing has happened yet to explain it to Nora.
hides: His growing distraction, without yet knowing himself what it's really about.
believes_falsely: That there's nothing yet worth explaining to Nora, since he doesn't yet understand what's bothering him.

knows: Julian has been distant and staying late these past several nights.
hides: Her quiet, growing worry — she hasn't voiced it to him.
believes_falsely: That it's simply work stress from the re-appraisal season, nothing more.

case.solution: Julian has not been unfaithful to Nora. His secrecy, late nights, and a hotel receipt Nora found are fully explained by his clandestine after-hours meetings with Anna Lin and a private lawyer to investigate the ledger discrepancies before deciding how — or whether — to tell Marguerite, combined with a personal loan he took out against their home to cover a debt payment called in by one of Marcus Tang's shell companies. He kept all of it from Nora because he panicked and didn't want to frighten her before he understood the scale of the problem himself. Nora, seeing secrecy, a woman, hotel receipts, and financial strain all at once, has drawn the reasonable but wrong conclusion that Julian is having an affair.
objective_facts: Julian Vantry met privately with Anna Lin on at least four occasions over six weeks, away from the auction house, to review ledger evidence without Marguerite's knowledge.
Julian Vantry took out a personal loan against the Vantry family home without telling Nora, to cover a debt payment called in by one of Marcus Tang's shell companies.
Nora Vantry found a hotel receipt from one of Julian's meetings with Anna Lin, dated the same week Julian became visibly withdrawn and evasive.
Julian Vantry has not been physically or emotionally unfaithful to Nora at any point.
Nora Vantry has not yet directly confronted Julian with the receipt, but has begun confiding her suspicions to Marguerite.

episodes.theme: Nora shows Julian the ledger discrepancy in confidence and he begins secretly meeting Anna Lin to understand its scope, Simon Lin recognizes Anna's name and face within days of her arrival but says nothing, and Marcus Tang quietly begins buying up the family's mortgage debt through shell companies.
episodes.intro_note: Nora found a signature in the 1951 ledger that doesn't belong to Augustin Vantry — and this morning, she's finally ready to show her husband what she's found.
episodes.outro_note: By nightfall, Julian carries a secret he can't yet explain, Simon holds a truth he still won't speak, and in a lawyer's office across town, Marcus Tang has already begun quietly buying pieces of the Vantry family's future.

case.solution: In 1938, Augustin Vantry partnered with Guangdong-born dealer Lin Wei-Chen to acquire and authenticate the forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art that became the Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection, under a signed agreement giving Lin a 40% stake and his name on every appraisal ledger. In 1951, facing a bank's due-diligence review for an expansion loan, Augustin forged thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal entries — stripping Lin's signature and replacing it with his own, and inflating the recorded appraisal sums by roughly sixty percent above their true 1938 figures. He used the inflated ledger to secure a larger loan than the collection actually justified, then used that loan to buy out Lin's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated valuation, effectively cheating Lin out of the true worth of his share while implicitly threatening to report him — an immigrant with little legal protection at the time — on a fabricated smuggling allegation if he resisted. Lin never publicly contested the buyout, but was quietly kept on staff in a demoted, uncredited restorer's role for the rest of his career and told his son Simon the full truth before he died in 1974. Simon Lin has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968, saying nothing, but has privately kept the original 1938 correspondence and several authentic pre-1951 ledger pages proving exactly what was altered.

case.solution: Marcus Tang, owner of the rival Meridian Auctions, has spent two years covertly acquiring the Vantry family's outstanding mortgage debt and buying out three minority-shareholding cousins through shell companies, working toward a controlling position in the house. His motive is only partly business: his grandfather, dealer Wing-Ho Tang, lost a contested 1952 bid against Augustin Vantry that was decided using the fraudulently inflated 1951 ledger valuation, and left behind papers documenting his suspicion of the result — a grievance Marcus inherited along with the family archive. Across town, Marcus Tang doesn't know what happened tonight. case.solution: In 1938, Augustin Vantry partnered with Guangdong-born dealer Lin Wei-Chen to acquire and authenticate the forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art that became the Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection, under a signed agreement giving Lin a 40% stake and his name on every appraisal ledger. In 1951, facing a bank's due-diligence review for an expansion loan, Augustin forged thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal entries — stripping Lin's signature and replacing it with his own, and inflating the recorded appraisal sums by roughly sixty percent above their true 1938 figures. He used the inflated ledger to secure a larger loan than the collection actually justified, then used that loan to buy out Lin's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated valuation, effectively cheating Lin out of the true worth of his share while implicitly threatening to report him — an immigrant with little legal protection at the time — on a fabricated smuggling allegation if he resisted. Lin never publicly contested the buyout, but was quietly kept on staff in a demoted, uncredited restorer's role for the rest of his career and told his son Simon the full truth before he died in 1974. Simon Lin has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968, saying nothing, but has privately kept the original 1938 correspondence and several authentic pre-1951 ledger pages proving exactly what was altered.

The inflated 1951 ledger was used to secure a bank loan that Augustin Vantry then used to buy out Lin Wei-Chen's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated 1938 valuation.
Lin Wei-Chen remained employed at the auction house afterward in an uncredited restorer role until his death in 1974, and never publicly disputed the buyout.

Lin Wei-Chen's son, Simon Lin, has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968 and privately holds original pre-1951 correspondence and several authentic ledger pages proving the forgery.

episodes.theme: CLIMAX & RESOLUTION — the full truth of the 1951 forgery becomes public record and the Vantry family reckons with what they owe the Lin family, Julian and Nora rebuild their marriage on the truth finally being spoken, Simon and Anna choose what kind of family they'll be going forward, and Marcus Tang's takeover bid is beaten back for now even as the auction house's financial survival is left deliberately open.
episodes.intro_note: Simon Lin's ultimatum still hangs over the house the morning after the gala: come forward within the week, or he will take the truth to the insurer and the press himself. With the deadline now the loudest voice in the room, Marguerite, Julian, and Nora face a decision the family has avoided for eighty-seven years — and Marcus Tang, sensing the ground shifting out from under his leverage, moves to close his deal before anyone else can set the terms.

case.label: The Forged Ledgers

knows: The full truth of the 1951 forgery, and that voluntary disclosure — not another week of containment — is now the only choice that preserves anything of the family's credibility.
The forgery's full history, and that the house's public statement will name Lin Wei-Chen and Augustin Vantry both, on the record, for the first time in the collection's history.
The full scope of the ledger forgery, presented to her at last alongside the rest of the family — including the true reason for Julian's months of secrecy.
The family has chosen to come forward before his deadline, and that the documents his father left him will finally enter the auction house's own public record.
Her professional re-appraisal and her grandfather's private documents now tell the same public story, for the first time without a division between the two.
The Vantry family is about to make the forgery public on its own terms, and that whatever leverage his partial knowledge gave him is evaporating in real time.
hides: The full extent of his own family's shell company holdings, even as his ledger leverage collapses — he isn't ready to reveal everything about his campaign just because theirs is now public.

case.solution: In 1938, Augustin Vantry partnered with Guangdong-born dealer Lin Wei-Chen to acquire and authenticate the forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art that became the Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection, under a signed agreement giving Lin a 40% stake and his name on every appraisal ledger. In 1951, facing a bank's due-diligence review for an expansion loan, Augustin forged thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal entries — stripping Lin's signature and replacing it with his own, and inflating the recorded appraisal sums by roughly sixty percent above their true 1938 figures. He used the inflated ledger to secure a larger loan than the collection actually justified, then used that loan to buy out Lin's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated valuation, effectively cheating Lin out of the true worth of his share while implicitly threatening to report him — an immigrant with little legal protection at the time — on a fabricated smuggling allegation if he resisted. Lin never publicly contested the buyout, but was quietly kept on staff in a demoted, uncredited restorer's role for the rest of his career and told his son Simon the full truth before he died in 1974. Simon Lin has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968, saying nothing, but has privately kept the original 1938 correspondence and several authentic pre-1951 ledger pages proving exactly what was altered.
objective_facts: The Vantry Auction House's Founding Collection — forty-seven lots of Chinese export porcelain and maritime art — was jointly acquired in 1938 by Augustin Vantry and his business partner Lin Wei-Chen under a signed partnership agreement giving Lin a 40% stake.

In 1951, thirty-one of the forty-seven original appraisal ledger entries were altered: Lin Wei-Chen's signature was removed and replaced with Augustin Vantry's, and the recorded appraisal sums were inflated by approximately sixty percent above the entries' original 1938 figures.
The inflated 1951 ledger was used to secure a bank loan that Augustin Vantry then used to buy out Lin Wei-Chen's 40% stake at its original, un-inflated 1938 valuation.
Lin Wei-Chen remained employed at the auction house afterward in an uncredited restorer role until his death in 1974, and never publicly disputed the buyout.

Lin Wei-Chen's son, Simon Lin, has worked at the Vantry Auction House as a framer and restorer since 1968 and privately holds original pre-1951 correspondence and several authentic ledger pages proving the forgery.

case.label: The Hidden Heir

knows: He and Anna have decided to be family in the open, no longer just a restorer and an appraiser who happen to share blood.
Simon Lin is her grandfather, publicly and personally now, and she has chosen to stay in Esmond Harbor rather than close the file and leave once her report is filed.
Simon Lin and Anna Lin are choosing to be public about their relationship, reframing fifty years of the house's own history in the process.
Simon and Anna's relationship is now open, and part of what the family owes the Lin family going forward.

case.solution: Anna Lin is Simon Lin's granddaughter and Lin Wei-Chen's great-granddaughter, though she grew up largely unaware of any connection to the Vantry Auction House. Her father, Simon's son, left Esmond Harbor in 1989 after a bitter argument with Simon over his father's lifelong refusal to challenge the Vantrys over the 1951 buyout, and cut off most contact afterward — leaving Anna with only fragments of family lore about 'a bad business deal' that cost her great-grandfather everything. She becomes a fine-art insurance appraiser and is independently assigned by the Vantry family's insurer — not by any design of her own — to reassess the Founding Collection ahead of a planned loan to an overseas museum. Genuinely by coincidence, not conspiracy, she recognizes the name Lin Wei-Chen in the original 1938 partnership paperwork and begins quietly researching her own family history alongside her professional appraisal, not yet realizing that the courteous old restorer at the house, Simon Lin, is her own grandfather. Simon recognizes her almost immediately by name and resemblance, but says nothing.
objective_facts: Anna Lin is the granddaughter of Simon Lin and great-granddaughter of Lin Wei-Chen, though she grew up unaware of any connection to the Vantry Auction House.

Anna Lin's father, Simon Lin's son, left Esmond Harbor in 1989 after a bitter argument with Simon about Simon's decision never to challenge the Vantry family over the 1951 buyout, and cut off most contact afterward.
Anna Lin was hired by the Vantry family's insurer, not by the Vantry family directly, to conduct an independent re-appraisal of the Founding Collection ahead of a planned loan to an overseas museum.
Independent of her professional assignment, Anna Lin has been quietly researching her family's history and possesses an old photograph of Lin Wei-Chen standing beside the Founding Collection in 1938.
Simon Lin recognized Anna Lin's name and resemblance to his own mother within days of her arrival at the auction house, but has not yet told her who he is.

case.label: The Hostile Buyout

knows: With the forgery public, Marcus Tang's threat of exposure no longer has anything to hold over the family — but the mortgage debt and cousins' shares he already holds are real and unresolved.
Marcus's leverage over the ledgers is gone, but the debt and shares he already controls are a separate problem that public honesty alone doesn't solve.

His attempt to force a discounted sale before disclosure has failed now that the Vantrys went public first, but he still holds the family's debt and three shares, and has no intention of walking away from either.
hides: His next move regarding the debt he still holds, from the Vantrys — this confrontation ends his current campaign, not his interest in the house.

case.solution: Marcus Tang, owner of the rival Meridian Auctions, has spent two years covertly acquiring the Vantry family's outstanding mortgage debt and buying out three minority-shareholding cousins through shell companies, working toward a controlling position in the house. His motive is only partly business: his grandfather, dealer Wing-Ho Tang, lost a contested 1952 bid against Augustin Vantry that was decided using the fraudulently inflated 1951 ledger valuation, and left behind papers documenting his suspicion of the result — a grievance Marcus inherited along with the family archive. Marcus has since obtained, through a private archive of his own, a partial copy of the 1951 ledger's inconsistencies — enough to suspect fraud, though not the full extent of it that Simon and Anna Lin can actually prove. Rather than exposing the forgery outright, he is using the private threat of exposure to pressure Marguerite into a quiet, deeply discounted sale before the museum-loan re-appraisal forces the truth into the open on its own.

Marcus Tang has made two private, undocumented approaches to Marguerite Vantry proposing an acquisition of the Vantry Auction House substantially below its market value.
Marcus Tang has not yet disclosed his knowledge of the forged ledgers to anyone outside his own staff.
